source_label: Murray N. Rothbard — The Mystery of Banking
excerpt: Throughout history, government has displayed a remarkable consistency in finding ways to debase the money — clipping coins, lowering precious-metal content, suspending convertibility, printing notes.
body:
Source excerpt:
Throughout history, government has displayed a remarkable consistency in finding ways to debase the money — clipping coins, lowering precious-metal content, suspending convertibility, printing notes.

---

Cited by leaf "What is sound money":
In the Austrian tradition, *sound money* is money that emerges from the market rather than being decreed by an authority. Carl Menger's account is the canonical one: people independently converge on whichever good has the best monetary properties — durability, divisibility, portability, recognisability, and above all difficulty of production — until that good becomes the medium of exchange [1]. Sound money is therefore a discovered institution, not a designed one.

Mises sharpens the definition in *The Theory of Money and Credit*: sound money is money whose supply is determined by the cost of producing it, not by the will of a central issuer [2]. Gold qualified historically because mining new gold to debase the stock was expensive enough that the supply grew slowly and predictably. A government IOU does not qualify, because the marginal cost of issuing one more unit is essentially zero — and what costs nothing to produce will eventually be produced in quantity [3].

The practical claim that follows is straightforward: when the issuer of money can create more of it without effort, they will, and the people closest to the spigot benefit at everyone else's expense. Rothbard argues at book length that every fiat regime in recorded history has followed this pattern — the temptation is structural, not personal [3]. Sound money removes the temptation by removing the discretion.

Bitcoin's claim to this category rests on a different mechanism: the supply schedule is enforced by every node, not by the cost of mining. New coins are produced by miners, but how many are produced and on what schedule is fixed in software that thousands of independent operators run and verify against. That is a stronger guarantee than gold's — gold's scarcity could in principle be overturned by an asteroid mining breakthrough; bitcoin's cannot be overturned without coordinated convincing of the operators themselves to change the rules. In Austrian terms, this places it firmly in the sound-money category that gold previously occupied alone.

---

Cited by leaf "Bitcoin as a unit of account and Gresham's law":
Money serves three classical functions: medium of exchange, store of value, and unit of account. Bitcoin clearly serves the first two for a meaningful and growing population of users — billions of dollars of value stored, millions of transactions per day. The unit-of-account question is more contested. Most prices in the world are still quoted in fiat, and even bitcoin-denominated services typically settle to a fiat reference at the moment of transaction.

The Austrian explanation for this lag has two parts. The first is *path dependence*: a unit of account is a coordination equilibrium, and switching costs are real. Sticky prices, accounting systems, tax codes, and customer expectations all anchor on whichever unit is currently dominant. Once enough of the economy denominates in dollars, an individual merchant who switches to bitcoin pricing imposes friction on their counterparties [1]. The second is *Gresham's law*: 'bad money drives out good' when both circulate as legal tender. Citizens hold the harder money and spend the softer one, because the soft money is what they want to be rid of and the hard money is what they want to accumulate [2]. This is exactly what we observe — bitcoin held, dollars spent.

Gresham's law is sometimes invoked as evidence that bitcoin will *never* become a medium of exchange and therefore never a unit of account. The Austrian counter is that Gresham's law assumes legal-tender parity between two monies — once that legal coercion lifts (or once one money's purchasing power degrades enough), the equilibrium flips and the harder money begins to dominate transaction flow. This is the historical pattern in every major hyperinflation: people abandoned the local fiat for a harder substitute as soon as the inflation became unbearable [3].

The order in which Bitcoin acquires its three monetary functions — store of value first, medium of exchange next, unit of account last — is therefore not a defect of Bitcoin's design but a *prediction* of standard monetary theory. The 'medium of exchange first' camp (associated with the Bitcoin Cash schism) argued the opposite ordering on engineering grounds; the 'store of value first' camp won out because the underlying economics pointed that way regardless of the engineering preferences.
